title: Abuse of Dominance ‚Äî Principles
content:
Session 5: Abuse of dominant position [¬ß¬ß4, 21, 27 and 28 of the Competition Act]  
 
Coal India v CCI [2023 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Coal India Limited (CIL) is a Government Company wholly owned by the CG, created pursuant to the Coal Mines 
(Nationalisation) Act, 1973.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Under the Nationalisation Act, the ownership, control, management and operation of coal mines vested in the CG and were 
entrusted to CI.  
‚û¢‚Äã As a result, CI enjoyed a statutory monopoly over the mining, production and distribution of coal in India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The objective of nationalisation was to reorganise and reconstruct coal mines to ensure rational, coordinated and scientific 
development of coal resources, consistent with the growing needs of the country, and to vest ownership and control of coal 
resources in the State so as to best subserve the common good, in furtherance of Article 39(b) of the Constitution. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Coal was additionally declared an essential commodity under the Essential Commodities Act, 1955, and the Nationalisation 
Act was placed in the Ninth Schedule (Article 31B) to protect it from judicial review.  
‚û¢‚Äã Consequently, CI emerged as a heavily protected statutory monopoly and a ‚Äúdarling commodity‚Äù supplier for 
decades. 
‚ùñ‚Äã However, over time, the regulatory and economic landscape changed: 
‚û¢‚Äã In 2007, coal was removed from the list of essential commodities. 
‚û¢‚Äã In 2017, the Nationalisation Act and its sister legislation were repealed. 
‚û¢‚Äã Liberalisation and public‚Äìprivate participation in coal mining weakened the rationale for absolute monopoly. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Proceedings were initiated by the CCI against CI for abuse of dominant position. 
‚û¢‚Äã CI challenged the jurisdiction of the CCI and the applicability of the Competition Act to it, arguing that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
It was a statutory monopoly created to fulfil constitutional goals under Article 39(b). 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Remedies under the Competition Act, particularly division under Section 28, conflicted with the 
Nationalisation Act, which required Central Government approval. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Therefore, CCI proceedings were purposeless and legally impermissible. 
 
117 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Competition Act, 2002 applies to a statutory body or government company created under Article 39(b) of the 
Constitution? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Coal India qualifies as an ‚Äúenterprise‚Äù under Section 2(h) and can be proceeded against for abuse of dominance 
under Section 4? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether mining and distribution of coal constitute sovereign functions, thereby excluding Coal India from the 
Competition Act? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether statutory monopolies and government companies fall within the scope of dominance analysis under Section 19(4)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether remedies under the Competition Act, particularly division under Section 28, are overridden or barred by the 
Nationalisation Act? 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Statutory Provisions 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 2(h), Competition Act: Defines ‚Äúenterprise‚Äù to include government departments and entities engaged in 
economic activity, excluding sovereign functions. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4: Prohibits abuse of dominant position. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 19(4): Enumerates factors to determine dominance; the CCI may consider ‚Äúany‚Äù or ‚Äúall‚Äù factors. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 19(4)(g): Explicitly includes monopoly or dominant position acquired by statute, government company, or 
PSU. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 28: Empowers CCI to order division of an enterprise abusing dominance. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 54: Allows the government to exempt enterprises from the Act. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 60: Non obstante clause giving overriding effect to the Competition Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Constitutional Context 
‚û¢‚Äã Article 39(b): Distribution of material resources of the community to subserve the common good. 
‚û¢‚Äã Article 12: Coal India qualifies as ‚ÄúState‚Äù. 
‚û¢‚Äã Article 31B / Ninth Schedule: Protection of Nationalisation Act (historical relevance). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Raghavan Committee Report 
‚û¢‚Äã Government enterprises should be within the ambit of competition law. 
‚û¢‚Äã Only sovereign functions deserve exclusion. 
‚û¢‚Äã State monopolies are not immune from competition scrutiny. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Applicability of the Competition Act to Government Companies 
‚û¢‚Äã Government companies are included within the ambit of the Competition Act. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Relying on the Raghavan Committee Report, the Court noted that exclusion of government enterprises 
has no justification except where sovereign functions are involved. 
‚û¢‚Äã Unlike the MRTP Act, which expressly excluded government agencies, the Competition Act contains no such 
blanket exclusion, reflecting clear legislative intent to subject government enterprises to competition law. 
‚û¢‚Äã Further, Section 54 empowers the government to exempt enterprises but CI did not enjoy any such exemption ‚Üí  
the Act necessarily applies. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Coal India is an ‚ÄúEnterprise‚Äù under Section 2(h) ‚Äì YES 
‚û¢‚Äã It is a government company, not a government department. 
‚û¢‚Äã It is engaged in economic activities‚Äîproduction and distribution of coal. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Under Section 2(i), ‚Äúgoods‚Äù include products that are manufactured, processed or mined; coal clearly 
qualifies. 
‚û¢‚Äã The exclusion in Section 2(h) applies only to sovereign functions such as defence, currency, atomic energy, and 
space.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Mining and distribution of coal cannot, by any stretch of imagination, be considered sovereign functions, 
particularly in light of public‚Äìprivate participation. 
118 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Statutory Monopoly and Article 39(b) 
‚û¢‚Äã CI argued that since it was created to fulfil Article 39(b), it should not be treated as an ‚Äúordinary monopoly‚Äù.  
‚û¢‚Äã The Court rejected this argument, holding that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Nothing in the Competition Act excludes monopolies created under statutes, even those pursuing 
constitutional goals. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Economic common good is a dynamic concept, not frozen in the socialistic vision of the 1950s. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Parliament was fully aware of the Nationalisation Act when enacting the Competition Act and 
consciously chose to include statutory monopolies within competition scrutiny. 
‚û¢‚Äã State intervention and monopolies may be justified historically, but that does not grant perpetual immunity from 
competition law. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Dominance under Section 19(4) 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 19(4) allows the CCI to rely on any one factor to establish dominance. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 19(4)(g) expressly treats statutory monopolies and government companies as relevant for dominance 
determination. 
‚û¢‚Äã Given the interplay of Sections 3, 5 and 11 of the Nationalisation Act, CI enjoyed an inevitable monopoly.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
A monopoly under Section 19(4)(g) is effectively treated as a dominant position ‚Üí Ci was correctly 
proceeded against under Section 4. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Conflict between the Nationalisation Act and the Competition Act (Section 28 Division) 
‚û¢‚Äã CI argued that remedies under the Competition Act‚Äîespecially division under Section 28‚Äîwere meaningless 
because the Nationalisation Act required Central Government permission. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court rejected this contention: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Section 60 (non obstante clause) gives an overriding effect to the Competition Act. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Parliament enacted the Competition Act with full knowledge of the Nationalisation Act. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Section 28 was deliberately included, even though it is a draconian and extreme remedy (and has rarely 
been used, even against firms like Google). 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction and remedial powers are not curtailed by the Nationalisation Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Abuse of Dominance (on facts) 
‚û¢‚Äã While the judgment primarily addresses jurisdictional and doctrinal questions, the Court accepted that CI‚Äôs 
conduct fell within the scope of Section 4, making it liable to competition scrutiny.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Court did not accept Coal India‚Äôs attempt to shield itself behind statutory creation or constitutional 
purpose. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Supreme Court held that: 
‚û¢‚Äã The Competition Act applies to statutory monopolies and government companies, including those created under 
Article 39(b). 
‚û¢‚Äã Coal India is an ‚Äúenterprise‚Äù under Section 2(h) and is engaged in economic activity. 
‚û¢‚Äã Mining and distribution of coal are not sovereign functions. 
‚û¢‚Äã Statutory monopoly itself is a relevant factor for dominance under Section 19(4)(g). 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI has jurisdiction to investigate Coal India for abuse of dominance under Section 4. 
‚û¢‚Äã Remedies under the Competition Act, including division under Section 28, are not barred by the Nationalisation 
Act due to Section 60. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The judgment firmly establishes that statutory monopolies are not immune from competition law and that constitutional 
objectives do not override market discipline under the Competition Act. 
 
WhatsApp [2024 | NCLAT] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã WhatsApp LLC, a wholly owned subsidiary of Meta Platforms Inc. (formerly Facebook Inc.), rolled out its 2021 Privacy 
Policy Update, making it mandatory for users to accept new terms and conditions to continue using WhatsApp. 
119 
 
‚û¢‚Äã The updated policy required users to consent to sharing of personalised user data (including service-related 
information, device information, and interactions) with Meta group companies, including Facebook, Instagram, 
and Messenger, ostensibly for business, advertising, and analytics purposes. 
‚û¢‚Äã Users were given a ‚Äútake-it-or-leave-it‚Äù choice 
‚ùñ‚Äã Given WhatsApp‚Äôs ubiquitous usage in India for personal, professional, and business communication, users were effectively 
locked-in with no meaningful choice to refuse data sharing. 
‚ùñ‚Äã WhatsApp/Meta contended that: 
‚û¢‚Äã Data protection issues are governed by the IT Act, 2000 and later the DPDP framework, which are lex specialis ‚Üí 
therefore, CCI lacked jurisdiction 
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI rejected this contention, holding that: 
‚û¢‚Äã The mere existence of parallel statutory regimes does not bar the CCI from exercising jurisdiction 
‚û¢‚Äã Competition concerns arising from data exploitation are within the CCI‚Äôs remit 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Commission examined: 
‚û¢‚Äã WhatsApp and Meta as a ‚Äúgroup‚Äù 
‚û¢‚Äã Their conduct across two relevant markets 
‚û¢‚Äã Abuse of dominance under Sections 4(2)(a)(i) and 4(2)(c) 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI has jurisdiction despite the existence of the IT Act/DPDP Act? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether WhatsApp and Meta constitute an ‚Äúenterprise‚Äù or ‚Äúgroup‚Äù under the Competition Act? 
‚ùñ‚Äã What is the correct definition of the Relevant Market(s)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether WhatsApp/Meta enjoy a dominant position in the identified markets? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the 2021 Privacy Policy amounts to abuse of dominance under: 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(a)(i) ‚Äì unfair conditions 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(c) ‚Äì denial of market access / leveraging 
‚ùñ‚Äã What remedies and penalties are appropriate? 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Competition Act, 2002 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(1) ‚Äì No enterprise or group shall abuse its dominant position 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2) ‚Äì Illustrative forms of abuse 
‚û¢‚Äã Explanation (b) to Section 4 ‚Äì Definition of ‚Äúgroup‚Äù 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 2(r), 2(s), 2(t) ‚Äì Relevant market, product market, geographic market 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 27 ‚Äì Orders and penalties 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 32 ‚Äì Extra-territorial jurisdiction 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 33 ‚Äì Interim measures 
‚ùñ‚Äã Key Doctrines Applied 
‚û¢‚Äã Single Economic Entity Doctrine 
‚û¢‚Äã Network Effects & Tipping 
‚û¢‚Äã Lock-in Effects & Switching Costs 
‚û¢‚Äã Multi-sided Market Analysis 
‚û¢‚Äã Data as a Source of Market Power 
‚û¢‚Äã Quality degradation as non-price abuse 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Jurisdictional Analysis: CCI vis-√Ý-vis IT Act / DPDP Act 
‚û¢‚Äã The preliminary objection raised by WhatsApp/Meta was that issues relating to data sharing and privacy fall 
within the exclusive domain of the Information Technology Act, 2000 and the emerging DPDP framework, 
rendering the CCI functus officio ‚Üí REJECTED. 
120 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Distinct harm addressed 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Data protection law regulates legality and consent AND competition law regulates market power, 
exploitation, and foreclosure 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
BUT ‚Üí the same conduct may violate multiple statutes simultaneously without ousting jurisdiction. 
‚û¢‚Äã Competition harm identified by CCI, not ‚Äúwhether consent exists‚Äù ‚Üí but whether consent is coerced due to 
dominance + whether data exploitation strengthens market power in adjacent markets 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Mere existence of a parallel regulator does not bar CCI ‚Üí what matters is whether conduct has an AAEC 
‚óè‚Äã
WhatsApp‚Äôs policy was assessed not as a privacy breach, but as exploitative and exclusionary 
conduct enabled by dominance. Hence, CCI validly assumed jurisdiction. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Enterprise, Group & Single Economic Entity Doctrine 
‚û¢‚Äã Group determination under Section 4 Explanation (b) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
WhatsApp LLC and Meta Platforms Inc. were examined as a ‚Äúgroup‚Äù, given: 100% ownership, 
centralised data governance, unified commercial and advertising strategy, interoperable data 
pipelines across platforms 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI applied the Single Economic Entity doctrine, recognising that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Legal separateness is irrelevant when entities do not act independently + pursue a common economic 
objective 
‚û¢‚Äã Data collected by WhatsApp is not siloed + actively leveraged across Meta‚Äôs ecosystem ‚áí conduct was attributable 
to the Meta Group as a whole, allowing dominance and abuse to be assessed collectively, not entity-wise. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Relevant Market Analysis ‚Äì Demand-Side Substitutability 
‚û¢‚Äã OTT Messaging Apps Market 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission adopted a consumer-centric substitutability test, focusing on: characteristics, intended 
use, functional substitutability, and user perception. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
OTT messaging apps form a distinct market ‚Üí provide bundled, multi-functional services: real-time 
messaging, voice/video calls, group chats, presence indicators, and media sharing 
‚óè‚Äã
These features are cumulatively non-substitutable with: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
SMS (limited functionality) 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Email (asynchronous, formal) 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Social media feeds (non-private communication) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Exclusion of proprietary ecosystems ‚Üí Apps like iMessage and FaceTime were excluded because: 
restricted to Apple devices ‚Üí lack interoperability + create ‚Äúwalled gardens‚Äù 
‚óè‚Äã
Consumers using Android devices cannot substitute these services, making them functionally 
and economically non-interchangeable. 
‚û¢‚Äã Rejection of ‚ÄúUser Attention‚Äù Market Argument 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
WhatsApp‚Äôs attempt to define a broad ‚Äúconsumer communication services‚Äù or ‚Äúuser attention‚Äù market 
was rejected for doctrinal reasons: 
‚óè‚Äã
Attention is not demand substitutability 
‚óè‚Äã
Services serving different consumer needs cannot be grouped merely because they compete for 
time 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Accepting this logic would: 
‚óè‚Äã
Collapse all markets into one 
‚óè‚Äã
Undermine the very concept of relevant market 
‚û¢‚Äã OTT messaging apps constitute a narrow, defensible relevant market, insulated from OTT entertainment, social 
media, or micro-blogging platforms. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Dominance Assessment in OTT Messaging Market 
‚û¢‚Äã Quantitative Indicators 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
WhatsApp‚Äôs MAUs and DAUs vastly exceed competitors 
‚óè‚Äã
DAU/MAU ratio close to unity ‚Üí habitual, indispensable usage 
‚óè‚Äã
Download figures show order-of-magnitude dominance 
121 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Qualitative Indicators (Decisive) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Network Effects 
‚óè‚Äã
Direct network effects: utility increases as more contacts join 
‚óè‚Äã
Indirect network effects: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Businesses adopt WA Business APIs due to user base 
‚óÜ‚Äã
This creates a self-reinforcing feedback loop: Users ‚Üí attract businesses ‚Üí increase 
utility ‚Üí attract more users 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Lock-in & Switching Costs ‚Üí switching costs are non-monetary but substantial: 
‚óè‚Äã
Loss of group chats 
‚óè‚Äã
Fragmented communication 
‚óè‚Äã
Need to coordinate mass migration 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No interoperability ‚Üí as user intensity increases, switching becomes irrational, even if alternatives exist. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Tipping & Entry Barriers 
‚óè‚Äã
Once critical mass is achieved: 
‚óè‚Äã
New entrants cannot compete on network size 
‚óè‚Äã
Market tips towards the dominant platform 
‚óè‚Äã
Results in ‚Äúwinner takes most‚Äù dynamics 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Multi-homing rebuttal ‚Üí although WhatsApp argued users can multi-home: 
‚óè‚Äã
Actual usage of parallel apps is minimal 
‚óè‚Äã
Network effects neutralise competitive pressure 
‚óè‚Äã
Multi-homing does not discipline dominance 
‚óè‚Äã
WhatsApp enjoys durable dominance, not merely transient popularity. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Dominance in Online Display Advertising Market 
‚û¢‚Äã Data as a Competitive Asset 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Meta possesses: cross-platform user data; behavioural, demographic, and interest profiling; and granular 
targeting capability 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This data is: non-replicable, accumulated over years, protected within a closed ecosystem 
‚û¢‚Äã Structural Entry Barriers ‚Üí Meta‚Äôs position makes effective entry commercially unviable ‚Üí a new entrant must: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Attract users at scale 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Generate engagement 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Build data history 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Develop targeting infrastructure 
‚ùñ‚Äã Economies of Scale & Scope 
‚û¢‚Äã Marginal cost per additional user ‚Üí near zero 
‚û¢‚Äã Shared infrastructure across platforms 
‚û¢‚Äã Data reuse across services 
‚û¢‚Äã Meta holds entrenched dominance in online display advertising. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Abuse under Section 4(2)(a)(i): Unfair Conditions 
‚û¢‚Äã Take-it-or-leave-it consent as exploitation 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The updated policy imposed: mandatory acceptance; no opt-out; no granular consent 
‚óè‚Äã
Such consent is illusory, as: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Users depend on WhatsApp for daily communication 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Exit is not realistically possible 
‚û¢‚Äã Vagueness & Unilateral Expansion 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Terms like: ‚ÄúService-related information‚Äù & ‚ÄúInteractions with others‚Äù are undefined, allowing: 
‚óè‚Äã
Expansive interpretation 
‚óè‚Äã
Future unilateral data expansion 
‚û¢‚Äã Quality Degradation Doctrine 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Competition law recognises: 
122 
 
‚óè‚Äã
Reduction in quality ‚âÝ price increase 
‚óè‚Äã
Excessive data extraction = lower quality of service 
‚óè‚Äã
Forced data sharing amounts to exploitative abuse. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Abuse under Section 4(2)(c): Denial of Market Access 
‚û¢‚Äã Leveraging dominance across markets ‚Üí by combining WhatsApp data with: facebook, instagram, and meta: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Enhances ad targeting precision 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Attracts advertisers 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Forecloses rivals 
‚û¢‚Äã Advertiser Lock-in ‚Üí advertisers face: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Higher switching costs 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Inferior alternatives 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Loss of targeting efficiency if they exit 
‚ùñ‚Äã Envelopment Strategy 
‚û¢‚Äã Meta bundles: communication services, advertising markets, and data assets ‚Üí denies rivals access to critical inputs 
(data), reinforces dominance across markets 
‚û¢‚Äã Conduct results in exclusionary abuse and market foreclosure. 
 
Google Mobile Ecosystem Case [NCLAT | 2023] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Industry Structure & Background 
‚û¢‚Äã The case concerns the Android mobile ecosystem in India, involving multiple stakeholders: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Google (provider of Android OS, app store, search engine, browser, YouTube, advertising services), 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers), 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
App developers, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
End users/consumers. 
‚û¢‚Äã Android is an open-source operating system, but access to Google Mobile Services (GMS)‚Äîa proprietary suite 
including Google Play Store, Google Search, Chrome, YouTube, Google Maps, Gmail, etc.‚Äîis governed by a set of 
contractual arrangements imposed by Google. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Key Agreements 
‚û¢‚Äã MADA (Mobile Application Distribution Agreement) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
OEMs wishing to pre-install any Google proprietary app must pre-install the entire GMS suite. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Obligations include default placement and premium positioning of Google Search and Chrome. 
‚û¢‚Äã AFA / ACC (Anti-Fragmentation Agreement / Android Compatibility Commitment) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
OEMs are restrained from manufacturing or selling devices running on Android forks (alternative 
versions of Android). 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Compliance with Google‚Äôs compatibility standards is mandatory across all devices manufactured or 
marketed by OEMs. 
‚û¢‚Äã RSA (Revenue Sharing Agreement) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
OEMs receive monetary incentives for ensuring exclusive default status of Google Search. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The purpose is to protect Google Search, the principal revenue-generating app for Google. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Allegations 
‚û¢‚Äã Mandated exclusive pre-installation of its apps as a condition for access to Play Store; 
‚û¢‚Äã Tied and bundled multiple Google apps with Play Store, despite them being available independently; 
‚û¢‚Äã Leveraged dominance in one market to protect or enter others; 
‚û¢‚Äã Foreclosed rival app stores, browsers, search engines, and online video platforms; 
‚û¢‚Äã Restricted development of Android forks, thereby limiting technical and scientific development; 
‚û¢‚Äã Created a ‚Äúwalled garden‚Äù ecosystem, reinforced by network effects, feedback loops, and data advantages. 
 
Issues: 
123 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã What are the relevant markets, and is Google dominant in them? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Google‚Äôs conduct under MADA, AFA/ACC, and RSA constitutes abuse of dominance under: 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(a)(i) ‚Äì unfair conditions, 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(b)(ii) ‚Äì limiting technical/scientific development, 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(c) ‚Äì denial of market access, 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(d) ‚Äì supplementary obligations, 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(e) ‚Äì leveraging. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether effects analysis is mandatory under Section 4. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether remedies and penalties imposed under Section 27 were justified and lawful. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether penalty could be imposed on a ‚Äúprovisional‚Äù basis. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Statutory Provisions 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(1): Prohibits abuse of dominant position. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(a)(i): Imposition of unfair or discriminatory conditions. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(b)(ii): Limiting or restricting technical or scientific development. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(c): Denial of market access ‚Äúin any manner‚Äù. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(d): Imposing supplementary obligations unrelated to the contract. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(e): Leveraging dominance in one market to enter or protect another. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 27(b): Penalty up to 10% of average relevant turnover. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Doctrinal Principles 
‚û¢‚Äã ‚ÄúThe bigger you are, the greater your responsibility‚Äù ‚Äì special responsibility of dominant enterprises.Tying involves 
a tying product (the must-have product) and a tied product (available independently). 
‚û¢‚Äã Abuse under Section 4 focuses on exclusionary and exploitative effects. 
 
Application by the CCI (Order dated 20 October 2022) 
‚ùñ‚Äã After delineating multiple relevant markets and finding Google to be dominant in each of them, the CCI applied Section 4 
by examining whether Google‚Äôs contractual arrangements and conduct amounted to abuse of dominance through 
exclusionary and exploitative practices. [marked by a structural analysis of the Android ecosystem, rather than an isolated 
assessment of individual agreements.] 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mandatory Pre-installation & Premium Placement of GMS (s. 4(2)(a)(i), 4(2)(d), 4(2)(c)) 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI held that under MADA, OEMs were compelled to pre-install the entire bouquet of Google Mobile 
Services (GMS) as a condition for licensing the Play Store.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Importantly, OEMs were given no choice either with respect to: 
‚óè‚Äã
which Google apps to pre-install, or 
‚óè‚Äã
where these apps would be placed on the device home screen. 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI applied Section 4(2)(a)(i) by holding that such mandatory bundling and placement constituted the 
imposition of unfair conditions on OEMs, who lacked countervailing buyer power due to Google‚Äôs 
indispensability.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
OEMs operate in a highly competitive downstream market and cannot realistically refuse Google‚Äôs terms 
without losing market viability. 
‚û¢‚Äã Further, these obligations were characterised as supplementary obligations unrelated to the subject matter of the 
contract, attracting Section 4(2)(d).  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The licensing of an app store did not objectively require compulsory installation of multiple proprietary 
Google applications. 
‚û¢‚Äã Resulted in denial of market access under Section 4(2)(c) to competing search engines, browsers, and video-hosting 
platforms.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Given Google‚Äôs default status and premium placement, rival applications were foreclosed from effective 
distribution channels at the very first point of consumer interaction. 
124 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Tying of Google Search with Play Store (s. 4(2)(e)) 
‚û¢‚Äã Google leveraged its dominance in the market for app stores for Android OS to protect and reinforce its dominance 
in the market for general web search services.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Although Google Search and Play Store operate in distinct relevant markets, their contractual tying under 
MADA and RSAs created de facto exclusivity. 
‚û¢‚Äã RSAs incentivised OEMs to ensure Google Search was set as the default across all search access points.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
While theoretically non-exclusive, the economic incentives made it commercially irrational for OEMs to 
choose alternative search providers ‚Üí to leveraging under Section 4(2)(e), since Google extended its 
dominance into adjacent markets by non-competitive means. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Tying of Play Store with YouTube and Chrome (s. 4(2)(e)) 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission applied similar reasoning to the tying of: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
YouTube with Play Store, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Chrome browser with Play Store. 
‚û¢‚Äã With respect to YouTube, the CCI found that compulsory pre-installation and premium placement gave YouTube 
an undue competitive advantage in the online video hosting platform (OVHP) market, characterised by strong 
network effects and high entry barriers ‚Üí constituted leveraging under Section 4(2)(e). 
‚û¢‚Äã Although the CCI initially found similar concerns regarding Chrome, this finding was later partially set aside by 
the NCLAT (discussed below). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Anti-Fragmentation Agreements (AFA/ACC) and Restriction on Android Forks (s. 4(2)(b)(ii)) 
‚û¢‚Äã Google‚Äôs AFA/ACC restricted OEMs from developing or selling devices running on Android forks unless they 
complied with Google‚Äôs compatibility requirements across all devices. 
‚û¢‚Äã Applying Section 4(2)(b)(ii), the Commission found that this conduct: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
limited technical and scientific development, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
reduced innovation in alternative operating systems, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
entrenched Google‚Äôs dominance by eliminating Android forks as a competitive constraint. 
‚û¢‚Äã The denial of Play Services APIs to non-compliant devices further reinforced entry barriers and foreclosed 
potential competition. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Ecosystem-Level Assessment 
‚û¢‚Äã MADA, RSA, and AFA/ACC were inter-related and inter-woven agreements, whose cumulative effect had to be 
assessed together ‚Üí the abuse was not in market entry per se, but in the manner of entry and expansion, which 
distorted competition across multiple markets. 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI repeatedly invoked the principle that dominant enterprises bear a ‚Äúspecial responsibility‚Äù to ensure that 
their conduct does not cause markets to tip irreversibly in their favour. 
 
Application by the NCLAT (Judgment dated 29 March 2023) 
‚ùñ‚Äã On appeal, the NCLAT largely upheld the CCI‚Äôs findings on dominance and abuse, but introduced important doctrinal 
refinements, particularly concerning effects analysis, remedies, and penalties. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Requirement of Effects Analysis under Section 4 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLAT accepted Google‚Äôs submission that abuse of dominance under Section 4 requires proof of anti-competitive 
effects, or at least a reasoned analysis demonstrating actual or likely harm to competition.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Relying on EU jurisprudence, the Tribunal held that dominance alone is insufficient ‚Üí there must be an 
assessment of whether the impugned conduct is anti-competitive in effect. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, the NCLAT concluded that the CCI had, in fact, conducted such an effects analysis by demonstrating: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
foreclosure of distribution channels for rival apps, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
reduction of consumer choice, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
reinforcement of entry barriers through network effects and data advantages, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Long-term market tipping risks. 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, the Tribunal upheld the findings of abuse under Sections 4(2)(a), 4(2)(b), 4(2)(c), and 4(2)(e). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Confirmation of Abuse but Partial Modification of Remedies 
125 
 
‚û¢‚Äã While affirming most findings, the NCLAT struck down certain remedies that it found to be disconnected from 
the established abuse.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Notably, it held that the CCI‚Äôs direction requiring Google to allow rival app stores to be distributed 
through the Play Store lacked a corresponding finding that Google had abused dominance vis-√Ý-vis app 
developers or rival app stores. 
‚û¢‚Äã Thus, while the finding of abuse survived, the scope of remedies was narrowed to ensure proportionality and 
doctrinal consistency. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Penalty and ‚ÄúProvisional Penalty‚Äù 
‚û¢‚Äã The NCLAT upheld the 10% penalty (‚Çπ1337.76 crore) based on the concept of relevant turnover, agreeing with the 
CCI that Google‚Äôs entire Android ecosystem contributed to revenue generation through data and traffic. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, it rejected the concept of a ‚Äúprovisional penalty‚Äù, holding that Section 27(b) does not contemplate 
revisable penalties.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Emphasising the need for finality and certainty, the Tribunal deleted the term ‚Äúprovisional‚Äù and held that 
penalties must be final once imposed. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Google is dominant in multiple interlinked markets of the Android mobile ecosystem in India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Through MADA, AFA/ACC, and RSA, Google abused its dominant position under Sections 4(2)(a)(i), 4(2)(b)(ii), 4(2)(c), 
4(2)(d), and 4(2)(e). 
‚ùñ‚Äã The abuses resulted in market foreclosure, denial of choice, and stifling of innovation. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Behavioural remedies were largely upheld, with limited modification by NCLAT. 
‚ùñ‚Äã A penalty of ‚Çπ1,337.6 crore was upheld, with clarification that penalties under Section 27(b) must be final.
